md: When you have **two events** ($A$ and $B$), you can ask:
**$A$ AND $B$:** both happen. (Intersection.)
**$A$ OR $B$:** at least one happens. (Union.)
Computing these requires being careful about whether the events are **independent** or **mutually exclusive** — these are *different concepts* and easy to confuse.
## 1. Independent Events — Multiply for AND
Two events are **independent** if the outcome of one doesn't affect the other.
**Examples of independent events:** Flipping a coin twice. Rolling a die, then drawing a card.
**Examples of dependent events:** Drawing two cards from a deck *without replacement* (the first draw changes the second's probabilities).

blocks.tex: P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad (\text{if independent})

md: **Example.** Flip a coin and roll a die.
$P(\text{H and rolling 4}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{12}$.
## 2. Dependent Events
When events depend on each other, multiply the probability of $A$ by the probability of $B$ **given $A$ already happened**.

blocks.tex: P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)

md: **Example — drawing without replacement.** A bag has 3 red and 5 blue marbles. Draw 2 without replacement.
$P(\text{first red}) = 3/8$.
$P(\text{second red} \mid \text{first red}) = 2/7$ (one red gone, one fewer marble total).
$P(\text{both red}) = \dfrac{3}{8} \cdot \dfrac{2}{7} = \dfrac{6}{56} = \dfrac{3}{28}$.

blocks.title: With vs without replacement
blocks.body: **With replacement** = independent. The marble goes back, probabilities reset. **Without replacement** = dependent. Each draw shrinks the pool.

md: ## 3. Mutually Exclusive — Add for OR
Two events are **mutually exclusive** (or **disjoint**) if they cannot both happen at the same time.
**Examples:** Rolling a 3 and rolling a 5 on the same die. Drawing a heart and drawing a spade in one draw.

blocks.tex: P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) \quad (\text{if mutually exclusive})

md: **Example.** Roll one die. $P(\text{2 or 5}) = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3$.
## 4. The General OR Rule
What if $A$ and $B$ *can* both happen? Then adding $P(A) + P(B)$ counts the overlap twice. Subtract it back:

blocks.tex: P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)

md: **Example.** Draw one card from a 52-card deck.
$P(\text{heart or face card}) = P(\text{heart}) + P(\text{face}) - P(\text{heart and face})$
(There are 3 face cards that are hearts: J, Q, K of hearts.)

blocks.title: Mutually exclusive vs independent — DIFFERENT
blocks.body: **Mutually exclusive:** can't both happen. $P(A \text{ and } B) = 0$. Use for OR (just add). **Independent:** one doesn't affect the other. $P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Use for AND (multiply). Don't confuse them. In fact, two events with positive probability are mutually exclusive iff they're NOT independent.

md: ### Worked example — tree diagram
Flip a coin twice. List all outcomes and find $P(\text{both heads})$ and $P(\text{at least one tail})$.
**Tree:**
First flip: H or T (each 1/2).
Second flip: H or T (each 1/2).
Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT — each $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4}$.
$P(\text{both heads}) = P(HH) = 1/4$.
$P(\text{at least one tail}) = 1 - P(HH) = 1 - 1/4 = 3/4$.
